cards_stock.narrative: The Apr 2026 debit card rebalancing — a record 0.69pp private gain and 1.29pp PSB surge — has diverged in May: privates held their ground at 25.88% (the highest in 29 periods), while PSBs gave back 0.21pp to 63.97%, partially unwinding the Apr spike. The minimal private gain (+0.05pp) and the PSB reversal suggest the Apr event was a stock cleanup (inactive card purges) rather than a demand shift, but privates have now sustained above 25.5% for two periods, confirming a structural break from the prior 25.1%–25.4% equilibrium. PSBs remain 0.80pp above their Jan–Feb 2026 floor but have not stabilised — if they fall below 63.5% in the next two periods, the 14-month erosion trend resumes. For lenders: privates have locked in a higher debit base with stronger transaction potential; PSB-focused strategies should plan for a 63.0%–64.0% share band, not a recovery above 64.5%.

infrastructure.narrative: India's acceptance infrastructure is undergoing a structural shift: UPI QR codes are scaling exponentially while POS terminals contract. UPI QR deployment hit 779.8M codes in May 2026, adding 9.0M units in a single month — 115× the 78K POS terminals added in the same period. The UPI-to-POS ratio reached 67.7×, the highest on record, while POS terminals posted their first YoY contraction at -0.5%, breaking a 17-period growth streak. Lenders financing merchant acceptance infrastructure should allocate capital exclusively to UPI QR-enabled solutions — POS is a declining asset class with no recovery signal, while UPI QR is on track to exceed 800M codes by Q3 2026. The 139× UPI-to-Bharat QR ratio confirms Bharat QR is commercially obsolete.

credit_card_txn.narrative: Credit card POS transaction volume grew 23.1% YoY in May 2026, recovering from the 20.8% low in Apr 2026 and returning to near-median growth. Underwriting and rewards strategies should maintain balanced exposure across physical and digital merchant networks.
